name: Route 3: Montón de Trigo
description:
The principle of this circuit is simple: a return trip to Montón de Trigo, the emblematic summit of the Guadarrama mountain range. The departure is on a climb which climbs gently. You follow the road which goes north from Cercedilla for a little over two kilometers.

You then join trails more suited to your trail runner profile. The slope steepens and the asphalt gives way to earth and stones. But not only that, since you are treading on an ancient Roman roadway which requires you to run on the cobblestones. Think of the ancient Romans who used it to reach northern Spain via the Puerto de la Fuenfría.

For your part, once you reach this mountain pass, you continue to climb. In the distance, you see a strange pile of stone taking shape, it is the Montón de Trigo, which as its Spanish name suggests, looks like a “heap of wheat”.

Your trail circuit then transforms into a skyrunning course. The terrain becomes very technical and steep, the paths disappear and you must pay attention to the markings to guide you. The scarcity of vegetation exposes you to the harsh climate. Until the summit, the height difference continues to increase. Once at the top, admire the superb panorama. You dominate the massif, as well as the Iberian Meseta on both sides, towards Madrid and Segovia.

All you have to do is go back down the same technical slope by which you climbed to Puerto de la Fuenfría. To return to the starting point, this time take the GR 10 to Collado de Marichiva. Admire the magnificent unobstructed views of the pass one last time, then head into the forest and let yourself be carried by the slope to the finish.
region_intro: Jogging around Cercedilla offers diverse terrain within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, an hour from Madrid. The region features extensive Scots pine forests, providing shaded routes and a refreshing environment. Its geological composition of granite and gneiss creates varied landscapes, including the Fuenfría Valley with its dense forests and mountain streams. Ancient Roman roads and bridges also offer historical paths for running. Best jogging routes around Cercedilla The most popular jogging route is Bonus route: Maratón Alpino Madrileño, a 28.6 miles (46.0 km) trail that takes 7 hours 58 minutes to complete. This challenging route features significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local runners is Route 4: Siete Picos, a difficult 11.7 miles (18.9 km) path. This route offers views of the Siete Picos and traverses varied mountain scenery. Local runners also love the Fuenfría Valley Loop, a 7.6 miles (12.2 km) trail leading through dense pine forests and along mountain streams, often completed in about 1 hour 42 minutes. Jogging around Cercedilla is defined by Scots pine forests, granite and gneiss terrain, and historical Roman roads. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy paths to challenging mountain routes. The routes in Cercedilla are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 30 reviews. Nearly 2,000 runners have used komoot to explore Cercedilla's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Cercedilla?
A: Cercedilla offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 70 trails documented on komoot. These routes cater to various preferences, from challenging mountain runs to more moderate paths through pine forests.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier run in Cercedilla?
A: Yes, while many routes in Cercedilla are challenging, there are a few easier and moderate options available. The region boasts well-marked trails suitable for all levels, ensuring that even beginners can find enjoyable paths. For example, you can find routes that traverse the extensive Scots pine forests, offering a refreshing and less strenuous experience.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging routes around Cercedilla?
A: The jogging routes around Cercedilla offer diverse terrain. You'll encounter extensive Scots pine forests, providing ample shade and a refreshing environment. The geological makeup of the region, characterized by granite and gneiss, creates varied landscapes. Many routes feature significant elevation changes as they traverse mountainous areas within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park.

Q: Are there any running routes in Cercedilla that offer historical sights or landmarks?
A: Absolutely! Cercedilla is rich in history. You can jog along stretches of an ancient Roman road, passing by well-preserved Roman bridges such as Puente de la Venta, Puente del Descalzo, and Puente de En Medio. This offers a unique blend of nature and history during your run.

Q: What are some notable natural features or viewpoints I can experience on a run in Cercedilla?
A: Cercedilla's running routes are rich with natural beauty and stunning viewpoints. You can explore the Fuenfría Valley, known for its dense pine forests and mountain streams. Prominent peaks like the Siete Picos offer dramatic backdrops. Viewpoints such as Mirador Vicente Alexandre provide panoramic vistas of the towering mountain peaks, rewarding runners with breathtaking scenery. You might also encounter highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4225665" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navacerrada Pass&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4220056" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del León&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any long-distance or challenging running trails in Cercedilla?
A: Yes, Cercedilla is well-known for its challenging and long-distance running trails, especially within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. A prime example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1282944563/bonus-route-maraton-alpino-madrileno" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bonus route: Maratón Alpino Madrileño&lt;/a&gt;, a demanding 46 km trail with significant elevation gain. Another difficult option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1276608278/route-5-la-penota" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route 5: La Peñota&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 22 km.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Cercedilla?
A: The running routes in Cercedilla are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 30 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse terrain, the refreshing Scots pine forests, and the stunning mountain views. The blend of natural beauty and historical elements, like the Roman road, also receives positive feedback.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Cercedilla?
A: Yes, Cercedilla offers several circular running routes. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1233915744/fuenfria-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuenfría Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through dense pine forests and along mountain streams. The region also features well-marked trails, including circular options from Las Dehesas de Cercedilla to Ducha de los Alemanes and Mirador de los Poetas.

Q: Is Cercedilla a good destination for family-friendly running or walking?
A: Yes, Cercedilla offers options for families. The Las Dehesas Recreational Area provides a pleasant setting with meadows and picnic tables, suitable for easier walks or runs. The region's well-marked trails include routes that are less strenuous, allowing families to enjoy the natural beauty together. While many routes are challenging, there are paths that cater to a more relaxed pace.

Q: What is the best season for jogging in Cercedilla?
A: Cercedilla is a great destination for jogging year-round, but each season offers a different experience. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. The extensive Scots pine forests offer ample shade, making summer runs more comfortable. Even in winter, the region can be beautiful, though some higher elevation trails might require appropriate gear due to snow.

Q: Are there any specific regulations or permits needed for jogging in the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park?
A: While general access for jogging on established trails is usually permitted, it's always advisable to check the specific regulations of the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, especially for certain areas or during particular seasons. For detailed information on park rules and any potential permit requirements, you can consult the official park website or local visitor centers. You can also find general information about the park at &lt;a href="https://www.globalnationalparks.com/cercedilla/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;globalnationalparks.com&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: How accessible are the running routes in Cercedilla by public transport or car?
A: Cercedilla is conveniently located just an hour from Madrid, making it easily accessible. Many running routes can be reached by car, with parking available in and around the town and at popular trailheads like Las Dehesas. Public transport options, such as trains and buses from Madrid, also serve Cercedilla, allowing access to the starting points of various trails.
tips: The Roman road of the port of Fuenfría represents the best preserved road section of Roman origin in the Community of Madrid. Since its construction in the 1st century, it has been used for centuries by the peoples of the two plateaus to cross the peaks of the Sierra de Guadarrama, undergoing important improvement and maintenance reforms, such as the reconstruction of the bridges in the Madrid sector in 1728. The construction of an engineering work of such magnitude for the time meant an important environmental alteration in the landscape and an ecological impact on the mountain ecosystems, in the same way that it happens today when designing new roads and highways. At the opening of the roadway box, the Roman engineers ordered to cut down and burn large areas of forest to function and act safely on the ground, adapting the environment to the needs of the road and at the same time working safe from wild animals , especially bears and wolves, who mercilessly attacked the workers from the depths of the forest. The loss of forest masses caused a degradation of plant species, leaving the undergrowth highly exposed to erosion, which, by losing the plant cover, breaks the dynamics of the landscape and increases the risk of landslides in the rainy season and avalanches in winter.
The port of Fuenfría is a mountain pass that crosses the Sierra de Guadarrama, joining the Spanish provinces of Segovia and Madrid.
El Montón de Trigo with its 2161 m high is one of the most important and highest mountains in the Guadarrama mountain range.

waypoints.name: Roman Road of Puerto de la Fuenfría
